title: Aspects of the large scale geometry of Out(Fn)
speaker: Lee Mosher
abstract: The theory of hierarchically hyperbolic groups, due to Behrstock, Hagen, and Sisto, was developed by abstracting work of Masur and Minsky on mapping class groups. Study of the large scale geometry of the outer automorphism group Out(Fn)of a rank n free group  Fnhas advanced by applying hyperbolic hierarchy concepts, despite the fact that Out(Fn)is not hierarchically hyperbolic - its Dehn functiongrows too fast. Continuing in this vein, we discuss the Two Over All Theorem (joint work with Handel) which expresses an exponential flaring property of a ``foldable" homotopy equivalence between two rank n graphs, using distance in the free splitting complex of  Fnas the argument of the exponential function. We mention several applications of that theorem to the geometry of the outer space and of the free splitting complex, including a new proof of the exponential growth of the Dehn function of Out(Fn)(joint work with Lyman). These works all apply in the more general context of outer automorphism groups relative to free factor systems.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: HH and mapping-class analogies
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames Out(Fn) through analogies with hierarchically hyperbolic groups, especially mapping class groups, emphasizing coarse Lipschitz retracts of subsurface stabilizers and quadratic Dehn-function bounds as model consequences.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolic complexes for Out(Fn)
proof_stages.summary: After recalling delta-hyperbolic metric spaces and the curve-complex hierarchy for mapping class groups, the speaker surveys several hyperbolic complexes acted on by Out(Fn) and notes that their interrelations already look unlike a standard HH top term.

proof_stages.label: Coarse Lipschitz retracts and non-HH conclusion
proof_stages.summary: For a free product decomposition Fn=Fk*F(n-k), Handel–Mosher's coarse Lipschitz retract from Out(Fn) to the stabilizer Aut(Fk)×Aut(F(n-k)) yields exponential lower bounds for Dehn functions and hence shows Out(Fn), n≥3, is not hierarchically hyperbolic.

proof_stages.label: Pointed free splitting complex as an Aut-space candidate
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by the loss of kernel information when passing from Aut(Fk) to Out(Fk), the speaker constructs the pointed free splitting complex as the canonical bundle of Bass–Serre trees over the free splitting complex, giving a natural Aut(Fn)-complex.

proof_stages.label: Relative hyperbolicity conjecture and two-over-all theorem
proof_stages.summary: Although the pointed free splitting complex is not hyperbolic because it contains flats, the speaker states a relative-hyperbolicity conjecture and the two-over-all theorem, which gives exponential crossing growth for two edges under sufficiently distant foldable maps.

proof_stages.label: Application through Sol geometry
proof_stages.summary: A joint application with Robin Lyman uses the two-over-all theorem to obtain a coarse Lipschitz retract from Out(Fn) to Sol, giving a new stronger proof of exponential Dehn-function growth and suggesting that non-hyperbolic pieces may be needed in a weakened hierarchy theory.

proof_stages.label: Stallings-fold proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The proof idea factors a foldable map through a finite Stallings fold path and uses monotonic rank growth of edge images to force, beyond a bounded-distance alternative, first one and then two edges whose images cross all target edges many times.

proof_stages.label: Exponential flaring interpretation
proof_stages.summary: In response to a question, the theorem is related to Cannon-style exponential flaring criteria for hyperbolicity and to combination theorems, while the improper nature of the pointed-free-splitting fibration is identified as the remaining obstacle.

examples.name: Free products as relative Out groups
examples.note: Free products of groups are briefly mentioned as cases of the more general relative outer automorphism framework, but the talk then restricts to Out(Fn).
examples.engagement_reason: He only says free products of groups are examples of the more general relative outer automorphism setting and immediately says he will not go there.

examples.name: Surface cut along a simple closed curve
examples.note: A finite-type surface split by an annular neighborhood of a curve gives the mapping-class stabilizer/retract picture that visibly motivates the analogous Out(Fn) stabilizer theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He draws a surface cut along a simple closed curve, fattens it to an annulus, identifies the subsurfaces, writes the stabilizer exact sequence with the twist kernel, and discusses the resulting coarse Lipschitz retractions.

passes.reason: A surface split along an essential simple closed curve is a standard mapping-class-group decomposition used as an analogy, not a failure case.
A surface decomposed along a simple closed curve is a standard subsurface-decomposition example used to illustrate the mapping-class-group retract theorem.
Cutting a surface along a simple closed curve is a standard surface decomposition used to motivate the mapping-class-group retract theorem.

The cut surface is used as a typical mapping-class-group analogy for a stabilizer/retraction theorem.

examples.name: Dehn's algorithm for surface groups
examples.note: The classical linear Dehn-function behavior of surface groups is used only to situate the quadratic Dehn-function result for hierarchically hyperbolic groups.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites Dehn’s algorithm as Dehn’s proof that surface groups have linear Dehn function, but does not run or analyze the algorithm on a surface group.

passes.reason: Surface groups are cited via Dehn’s algorithm as having linear, hence minimal, Dehn function among finitely presented groups.

examples.name: Classical hyperbolic space and hyperbolic-plane flaring
examples.note: Riemannian hyperbolic spaces, later specialized to equidistant curves and circles in the hyperbolic plane, illustrate delta-thinness and exponential flaring rather than supplying a step of the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through hyperbolic-plane flaring by considering equidistant curves from a geodesic and circles about a point, giving the cosh/sinh length growth and relating this to exponential flaring and hyperbolicity.

passes.reason: Classical hyperbolic space is a maximally regular constant-curvature model used to explain exponential flaring, not to refute a claim.
